content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Joseph W. Brady, CCIM, SIOR 
President 
August 9, 2016 
Trustee at Victor Valley Community College District 
C/0 The Bradco Companies 
P O  Box 2710 
Victorville, CA 92395 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-130 
Dear Mr. Brady: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") docs not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
assistance. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on 
the provisions of the Act, and nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that 
may have already taken place. 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's campaign disclosure rules apply to the advertisement you intend to place for 
your business that provides commercial brokerage services? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As explained below, the Act's campaign disclosure provisions will apply to the 
advertisement you intend to place for your business. 
FACTS 
You are the President of Joseph Brady, Inc., dba The Bradco Companies in Victorville, 
California, which provides commercial brokerage services. You were appointed to the Board of 
Trustees at Victor Valley Community College in February 2011. You were then elected to the 
position in 2012. You intend to run for re-election to the Board. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9 JO 14. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Tule 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-130 
Page No. 2 
You would like to place an advertisement for your commercial brokerage business in certain 
newspapers and magazines that also references your position as a Trustee. You will use company 
funds to pay for the advertisements. 
You provided a proof for the advertisement that you intend to use, which displays your 
picture and the company name at the top. The advertisement has at least two photos of current 
commercial listings and what appear to be coupons for a report and a book offered by your 
company. The following statement is prominently displayed at the bottom of the advertisement: 
I am proud and /111111hled to he elected as a Trustee at Victm· Valley C01111111111ity College District 
Plea.\·e vote November 8, 2016 
This is 1101 a paid political advertisemelll 
Joseph W. Brady for Victor Valley Co1111111mity College Board o/Trm.1ees 2016 ID 1338996 
ANALYSIS 
You have generally asked about obligations under the Act that may arise from your 
advertisements in magazines or newspapers. 
A contribution is any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political purposes 
without full and adequate consideration. (Section 820 I 5; Regulation 18215.) A payment is made for 
political purposes if it is received by or made at the behest of a candidate, unless it is clear from the 
surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made for personal purposes unrelated 
to his or her status as a candidate or elected officer. (Regulation 18215{a)(2).) A payment is made at 
the behest of a candidate it if is made "under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, 
consultation, coordination, or concert" with the candidate. (Regulation 18225.?{b).) For purposes of 
the Act, an incumbent elected officer is considered a "candidate." (Section 82007.f 
This notwithstanding, a contribution docs not include an "expenditure made at the behest of 
a candidate in connection with a communication directed to voters or potential voters as part of 
voter registration activities or activities encouraging or assisting persons to vote, if the expenditure 
does not constitute express advocacy." (Regulation 1821 S(c)( 1 ).) For purposes of the Act, "express 
advocacy," refers to "a communication that •expressly advocates' the nomination, election or defeat 
of a measure ifit contains express words of advocacy such as "vote for," "elect," "support," "cast 
your ballot," "vote against," "defeat," "reject," "sign petitions for," or, within 60 days prior to an 
election in which the candidate or measure appears on the ballot, the communication otherwise 
refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that the communication, taken as a whole, 
unambiguously urges a particular result in an election. (Regulation I 8225(b )(2).) 
The advertisement at issue here will be published under your control and at your direction. It 
will include your photograph, name and status as a Trustee. It is unclear whether the advertisement 
will be published within 60 days prior to the election but, nonetheless, it can arguably be said that it 
1 Section 82007 further defines candidate, m pertinent part, as ... an individual who ... gives his or her consent 
for any other person to ... make an expenditure with a view to bringing about his or her nomination or election to 
elecuve office. whether or not the specific elective office which he or she will seek ... is known at the time the 
expenditure is made and whether or not he or she has announced his or her candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy 
at such time." 


File No. A-16-130 
Page No. 3 
expressly advocates for your re-election to the Board of Trustees. (See Roos Advice Letter, A-85-
057 [ crime prevention brochure which included the photograph, name and title of an elected officer 
and which was to be distributed in the elected officer's district constituted an in-kind contribution to 
the elected officer]; see also McCarthy Advice Letter, No. A-90-163 [ advertisements produced by a 
non-profit university to promote its professional studies program in which the name, title, and 
photograph of a candidate for elective office appear, even though no advocacy is made, are 
contributions to the candidate].) 
Moreover, Section 82015(c) provides: 
'"Contribution' includes . . .  the candidate's own money or property used on 
behalf of his or her candidacy other than personal funds of the candidate used to 
pay either a filing fee for a declaration of candidacy or a candidate statement 
prepared pursuant to Section 13307 of the Elections Code." 
And under the Act, the following apply: 
Any personal funds a candidate will use to promote his or her election shall be 
deposited into the campaign account prior to expenditure and all campaign 
expenditures shall be made from the account. (Section 85201(d).) 
The contribution limit provisions do not apply to a candidate's contributions of 
his or her personal funds to his or her own campaign. (Section 85301 (d); See 
Buckley, .. Valeo ( 1976) 424 U.S. l, 51.) 
In prior advice we stated: 
In the past, we have advised that since a corporation is for many purposes an 
entity that is distinct from its individual members or stockholders, a candidate 
who owns a corporation must treat the corporation as a separate legal entity. 
Consequently, the assets of the candidate's corporation were not considered 
the candidate's "personal funds" for purposes of Section 8530l(d) and are 
subject to the contribution limits of the Act. (See e.g., Srema11iak Advice 
Letter, No. A-03-092.) However, the Srema11iak conclusion that the use of 
one's own assets held by a wholly-owned corporation is subject to limits 
appears to be unsupported by controlling case law in the area. 
(Burch Advice Letter, No. A-14-032.) 
Thus, consistent with the above, we conservatively advise that the payments for the 
advertisements would be considered a contribution to you from your company.3 But such 
contributions to you from your wholly-owned company are not subject to any contribution limit that 
may apply. 
3 Note that the cost of the advertisement containing both political and nonpolitical material can be prorated. 
(See Wilmn Advice Letter. No. A-05-101 [the allocation may be based on the comparative amount of space devoted to 
the political and non-political material].) 


JW:jgl 
FilcNo. A-16-130 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Counsel, Legal Division
